title: Local LLM Server Vendor Overview: Eight Major Options Compared
content:
The local LLM server landscape in 2025-2026 has matured significantly, with eight major options covering the full spectrum from beginner-friendly desktop apps to high-throughput production servers. Understanding the fundamental distinctions helps narrow your choice before diving into detailed comparisons.

The eight major options fall into three categories:

**Desktop GUI Applications** (best for individual users and teams exploring locally): LM Studio, Jan.ai, GPT4All. These prioritize ease of use with visual model management, built-in chat interfaces, and one-click installation. They are not designed for headless or production server deployments.

**API Server Solutions** (best for integration and development): Ollama, LocalAI, llama.cpp server. These expose HTTP APIs and are designed to serve requests from applications. Ollama leads this category for developer experience; LocalAI leads for multi-modal breadth; llama.cpp server is the raw, minimal-overhead option.

**Production Serving Frameworks** (best for high-concurrency, multi-user enterprise): vLLM, text-generation-webui (oobabooga). These are designed for throughput at scale and handle dozens to hundreds of concurrent requests efficiently.

Key differentiators to evaluate:

1. **OpenAI API compatibility**: Critical if you want drop-in replacement for OpenAI SDK calls. Ollama, LocalAI, LM Studio, and Jan.ai all implement /v1/chat/completions. GPT4All only implements a subset and only on localhost. vLLM implements the full OpenAI API spec.

2. **Model format support**: GGUF has become the 2025-2026 standard for local deployment (supported by all eight). GPTQ and AWQ are GPU-quantized formats supported by vLLM and text-generation-webui but not well-supported by Ollama or LocalAI. SafeTensors/HuggingFace format is best handled by vLLM and TGI.

3. **GPU acceleration**: Ollama supports CUDA, Metal (Apple Silicon), ROCm (AMD). vLLM requires CUDA (NVIDIA only, no Metal). llama.cpp supports CUDA, Metal, Vulkan, OpenCL. LocalAI supports CUDA and CPU.

4. **Deployment model**: Docker-native (Ollama, LocalAI, vLLM), desktop-only (LM Studio, GPT4All), cross-platform binary (llama.cpp server, Jan.ai).

5. **Multi-model serving**: vLLM and LocalAI handle multiple models with proper isolation. Ollama can load multiple models but serializes access. LM Studio and Jan.ai are single-model-at-a-time.

This overview provides the foundation for the detailed per-vendor entries that follow.

title: Ollama: Deep Dive — Installation, Performance, and Production Use
content:
Ollama is the dominant local LLM server as of 2025-2026, winning on developer experience, ecosystem integrations, and the fastest path from zero to running model. It is the correct default choice for the majority of private AI deployments.

**Installation Complexity: 1/5 (trivial)**
macOS: `brew install ollama` or download .dmg. Starts as a launchd service automatically.
Linux: `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh` — one command, service configured.
Windows: Download .exe installer, runs as Windows service.
Docker: `docker run -d -p 11434:11434 ollama/ollama` — fully containerized.
Kubernetes: Helm chart available at github.com/otwld/ollama-helm.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF (primary, via llama.cpp backend), Ollama's own Modelfile format (wraps GGUF with system prompts and parameters). Does NOT natively support GPTQ, AWQ, or raw SafeTensors — models must be converted to GGUF or downloaded from the Ollama registry.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Near-complete. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/models. Drop-in replacement for most OpenAI SDK usage. Does not implement fine-tuning or assistants API.

**Performance (tokens/sec on typical hardware)**:
- Apple M3 Pro, 7B Q4_K_M: 45-55 t/s
- Apple M3 Max 36GB, 7B Q4_K_M: 70-85 t/s
- Apple M3 Max 36GB, 13B Q4_K_M: 40-55 t/s
- RTX 4090 24GB, 7B Q4_K_M: 120-150 t/s
- RTX 4090 24GB, 13B Q4_K_M: 65-90 t/s
- CPU-only (AMD Ryzen 9 7950X), 7B Q4_K_M: 8-15 t/s
Note: Ollama is 10-20% faster than LocalAI on identical hardware due to lower overhead.

**GPU Support**: CUDA (NVIDIA, auto-detected), Metal (Apple Silicon, best-in-class support), ROCm (AMD, experimental), Vulkan (limited), CPU fallback (always available).

**Multi-Model Serving**: Ollama can load multiple models simultaneously via OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS environment variable (default: 1 on CPU, 3 on GPU). Models are cached in memory and swapped. Concurrent requests to different models are serialized per model, not truly parallel.

**Context Window**: Supports extended context via num_ctx parameter in Modelfile or API. Models like Llama 3.1 run up to 128K context. Memory usage scales with context length.

**Best For**: Developer integrations requiring OpenAI drop-in replacement, Mac-native deployments (unmatched Metal support), single-user to small team deployments (1-10 concurrent users), CI/CD pipelines needing local model access, quick experimentation across many models via Ollama registry.

**Worst For**: High-concurrency production (>20 concurrent users), multi-GPU tensor parallelism, GPTQ/AWQ model formats, organizations requiring fine-grained resource quotas per user, GPU-only deployments (no Apple Silicon Metal-equivalent on Windows/Linux without NVIDIA).

**Critical Production Config**:
```bash
OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2
OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4
OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=30m
OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434
```

title: LocalAI: Multi-Modal, Multi-Backend Local AI Stack
content:
LocalAI positions itself as a comprehensive local AI stack rather than just an LLM server. It supports text generation, image generation (Stable Diffusion), audio transcription (Whisper), text-to-speech, and embeddings — all under one OpenAI-compatible API surface.

**Installation Complexity: 3/5 (moderate)**
Docker is the recommended and most reliable path: `docker run -p 8080:8080 localai/localai:latest-aio-cpu` for CPU, or the GPU variant with CUDA. The 'all-in-one' images include all backends pre-compiled. Manual installation requires building from source with backend-specific dependencies (llama.cpp, whisper.cpp, stable-diffusion.cpp), which can be complex.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF (via llama.cpp backend), GPTQ (via exllama backend), AWQ (via exllama2 backend), SafeTensors/HuggingFace (via transformers backend), ONNX, Diffusers (image), Whisper (audio). This is the broadest format support of any local server.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Excellent. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/images/generations (DALL-E equivalent), /v1/audio/transcriptions (Whisper equivalent), /v1/models. The most complete OpenAI-compatible local stack available.

**Performance**: LocalAI adds ~5-15ms overhead per request compared to raw llama.cpp due to its backend routing layer. Typical token generation:
- CPU, 7B Q4: 7-12 t/s
- RTX 4090, 7B Q4: 100-130 t/s (10-20% slower than Ollama due to overhead)
- RTX 4090, 7B with exllama2: 120-160 t/s (faster for GPU quantized formats)

**GPU Support**: CUDA (primary), HIPBLAS/ROCm (AMD), Metal (Apple Silicon, via llama.cpp backend, limited compared to Ollama), CPU with AVX/AVX2/AVX-512 auto-detection.

**Multi-Model Serving**: True multi-model support via backends. Each model can be assigned a different backend (llama.cpp, whisper, stable-diffusion). Concurrent requests handled with configurable parallel workers per backend.

**Context Window**: Limited by underlying backend (llama.cpp for GGUF = same as Ollama, up to 128K for supported models).

**Best For**: Organizations needing a unified API for text + image + audio under one endpoint, teams replacing the full OpenAI API surface (not just chat), multi-modal applications requiring image generation and transcription alongside LLMs, Docker-first or container-native environments.

**Worst For**: Apple Silicon users (Metal support trails Ollama significantly), beginners (no GUI, complex configuration), performance-critical single-model deployments (overhead vs raw Ollama/vLLM), Windows without Docker (complex native builds).

**Key Config (docker-compose)**:
```yaml
services:
  localai:
    image: localai/localai:latest-aio-gpu
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]
    environment:
      - PARALLEL_REQUESTS=true
      - BACKENDS=llama-cpp,whisper,stablediffusion
    volumes:
      - ./models:/build/models
```

title: LM Studio: Best Desktop Experience for Teams and Explorers
content:
LM Studio is the most polished desktop application for local LLM interaction, targeting business users and developers who want a GUI-first experience with an optional API server. It runs on macOS, Windows, and Linux.

**Installation Complexity: 1/5 (trivial)**
Download the installer from lmstudio.ai and run it. No command line required. Model discovery and download is built into the UI via a HuggingFace model browser. The API server is toggled on/off within the app.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF (primary, via llama.cpp backend). HuggingFace model search and download built into the UI. Does not directly support GPTQ or AWQ without conversion. MLX format supported on Apple Silicon for faster inference.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Complete for core endpoints. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/models. The local server binds to port 1234 by default. Drop-in for OpenAI SDK.

**Performance**: Similar to Ollama (both use llama.cpp under the hood) with 10-20% performance overhead from the GUI process. The GUI adds 500MB-1GB additional RAM consumption. On machines without dedicated GPUs, LM Studio sometimes outperforms Ollama due to better Vulkan offloading.
- Apple M3 Pro, 7B Q4: 40-50 t/s
- Apple M3 Max, 13B Q4: 35-50 t/s
- RTX 4090, 7B Q4: 110-140 t/s
- CPU (no GPU), 7B Q4: 7-12 t/s (Vulkan can help on AMD iGPU systems)

**GPU Support**: Metal (Apple Silicon — MLX backend available for faster inference), CUDA (NVIDIA), Vulkan (AMD/Intel iGPU, useful for CPU-primary systems with integrated graphics).

**Multi-Model Serving**: Single active model at a time through the GUI. API server serves whichever model is loaded in the UI. Not suitable for multi-model routing.

**Context Window**: Configurable per model up to the model's max. Displayed as slider in UI. Same llama.cpp limits apply.

**Best For**: Business users who are not developers (excellent GUI), teams evaluating many models quickly via built-in HuggingFace browser, macOS users who want MLX acceleration, Windows users with AMD GPUs (Vulkan support), offline demo environments, non-technical stakeholders who need to run AI locally without CLI.

**Worst For**: Headless/server deployments (requires a display), multi-model APIs, high-concurrency production, Docker/Kubernetes environments (desktop app, not container-native), CI/CD pipelines, GPTQ/AWQ format support.

**Important Limitation**: LM Studio's local server is tied to the desktop application being running. It cannot be used as a persistent background service in the same way as Ollama or vLLM.

title: Jan.ai: Open-Source ChatGPT Alternative with Agent Support
content:
Jan.ai is an open-source desktop application that directly competes with ChatGPT's interface while running locally. Its 2025 development focus on agentic workflows (Project workspaces, Browser MCP) differentiates it from LM Studio and GPT4All.

**Installation Complexity: 1/5 (trivial)**
Download from jan.ai, install as a native app. macOS, Windows, Linux (AppImage/deb). No CLI required. Model download and management is handled within the app.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF (primary, via llama.cpp/nitro backend). The app also supports connecting to remote providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, Mistral) as a unified interface — both local and remote from one UI.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Full. Jan exposes an OpenAI-compatible local server (default port 1337) that implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/models, /v1/embeddings. Can be used as a drop-in API server when the desktop app is running.

**Performance**: Similar to LM Studio (llama.cpp/nitro backend). The nitro engine is a performance-optimized fork of llama.cpp. Performance data:
- Apple M2 Pro, 7B Q4: 35-50 t/s
- Apple M3 Max, 13B Q4: 30-45 t/s
- RTX 4090, 7B Q4: 110-135 t/s

**GPU Support**: Metal (Apple Silicon), CUDA (NVIDIA), CPU fallback.

**Multi-Model Serving**: Single active model at a time. Supports switching between local and remote providers in the same interface.

**Context Window**: Standard llama.cpp limits, configurable in the UI.

**Best For**: Users who want a privacy-first ChatGPT replacement with local-first execution, teams wanting one interface for both local models and remote APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic), developers building agentic applications who want MCP integration in a local environment, privacy-conscious users who need to audit all network connections.

**Worst For**: Headless server deployments, high-concurrency API serving (desktop app limitation), GPTQ/AWQ formats, teams requiring enterprise authentication (SSO, LDAP), pure API-integration use cases (better served by Ollama).

**Distinctive Feature — Agentic Workspaces**: Jan's Project workspaces (introduced 2025) allow creating long-running agent contexts with file access and Browser MCP, making it the only desktop LLM app with first-class agent capabilities comparable to Claude Desktop's MCP support.

title: llama.cpp Server: Minimal, Maximum Control, Zero Overhead
content:
llama.cpp is the foundational inference engine that powers Ollama, LM Studio, Jan.ai, and LocalAI. Its built-in HTTP server (`llama-server`) gives you raw access to the engine without any abstraction layer, providing the lowest possible latency and the highest degree of control.

**Installation Complexity: 4/5 (complex)**
Building from source: `git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp && cd llama.cpp && cmake -B build -DGGML_CUDA=ON && cmake --build build --config Release -j8`. Requires build toolchain (cmake, gcc/clang). Pre-built binaries available on GitHub Releases for common platforms. Docker image available: `ghcr.io/ggerganov/llama.cpp:server-cuda`.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF exclusively (llama.cpp's own format). However, llama.cpp includes conversion scripts to convert from SafeTensors, GGML (legacy), and HuggingFace formats. The broadest GGUF quantization support of any engine: Q2_K through Q8_0, F16, BF16, plus K-quants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, etc.).

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Partial. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/models, /health. Does not implement all OpenAI parameters (some are no-ops). Has its own additional endpoints not in the OpenAI spec (/slots, /tokenize, /detokenize).

**Performance**: Fastest single-stream token generation of any option. No wrapper overhead.
- Apple M3 Max, 7B Q4_K_M: 80-100 t/s (benchmark baseline)
- RTX 4090, 7B Q4_K_M: 140-175 t/s
- RTX 4090, 70B Q4_K_M (tensor split): depends on VRAM available
- CPU (Ryzen 9 7950X, AVX-512), 7B Q4_K_M: 10-20 t/s

**GPU Support**: CUDA (LLAMA_CUDA), Metal (LLAMA_METAL — best Apple Silicon support), ROCm (LLAMA_HIP — AMD), Vulkan (LLAMA_VULKAN — any GPU), OpenCL (LLAMA_OPENCL — older hardware), CPU SIMD (AVX/AVX2/AVX-512/NEON — auto-detected).

**Multi-Model Serving**: Single model per server instance. Multiple models require multiple processes on different ports, coordinated by a proxy (nginx, HAProxy, or custom).

**Context Window**: Configurable via `-c` flag. Supports grouped-query attention (GQA) for extended context. Limited by available memory: each token in context consumes (n_layers × n_heads × head_dim × 2 × sizeof(float16)) bytes in the KV cache.

**Best For**: Performance benchmarking (the reference implementation), custom server builds where you control every parameter, embedded/IoT deployments needing minimal dependencies, research and fine-grained profiling, production deployments where you have DevOps capacity to manage the raw server, multi-GPU tensor parallelism via `--split-mode row`.

**Worst For**: Beginners (no GUI, complex setup), model format diversity (GGUF only), true multi-model serving from one instance, high-level orchestration without custom work, organizations without DevOps resources.

**Essential Flags**:
```bash
./llama-server \
  -m ./models/llama-3.1-8b-instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf \
  -c 32768 \
  --n-gpu-layers 99 \
  --port 8080 \
  --parallel 4 \
  --cont-batching \
  --flash-attn
```

title: vLLM: High-Throughput Production LLM Serving at Scale
content:
vLLM is the leading production LLM serving framework for organizations that need to handle tens to hundreds of concurrent requests efficiently. Its PagedAttention algorithm eliminates memory waste from KV cache fragmentation, enabling throughput that is 2-24x higher than naive serving.

**Installation Complexity: 3/5 (moderate)**
`pip install vllm` — Python package with CUDA dependencies. Requires NVIDIA GPU with CUDA 11.8+ (CUDA 12.x recommended). Docker: `docker run --runtime nvidia --gpus all vllm/vllm-openai:latest`. ROCm support (AMD) is available but experimental. NO Apple Silicon/Metal support — vLLM requires NVIDIA CUDA or AMD ROCm.

**Supported Model Formats**: HuggingFace SafeTensors (native), GPTQ (via AutoGPTQ integration), AWQ (via AutoAWQ), GGUF (experimental, performance may lag llama.cpp). Best format support for GPU-quantized inference at scale. Supports Mistral, Llama, Gemma, Qwen, Phi, DeepSeek architectures and many more.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Full. Most complete OpenAI API implementation of any open-source server. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/models, tool calling, structured outputs (JSON mode), logprobs, streaming. Often the first open-source server to match new OpenAI API features.

**Performance**: Dramatically higher throughput than all other options under concurrent load. PagedAttention + continuous batching allows requests to be processed without KV cache waste.
- RTX 4090, Llama-3.1-8B (FP16), 50 concurrent users: 800-1200 tokens/sec total throughput
- A100 80GB, Llama-3.1-70B (FP8), 20 concurrent users: 400-600 tokens/sec total
- H100 80GB, Llama-3.1-70B (FP8), 50 concurrent users: 1200-2000 tokens/sec total
- Single user (no concurrency): comparable to llama.cpp, sometimes 10-20% slower for per-request latency

**GPU Support**: NVIDIA CUDA (primary, excellent), AMD ROCm (experimental), Intel Gaudi (via Habana Labs backend), TPU (Google Cloud). DOES NOT support Apple Silicon Metal or CPU-only inference.

**Multi-Model Serving**: Designed for single-model serving with maximum throughput. Use separate vLLM instances for different models behind a router (LiteLLM, RouteLLM). The `--model` flag at startup sets the model. Multi-LoRA serving is supported (multiple LoRA adapters on one base model).

**Context Window**: Supports very long context via PagedAttention (no memory fragmentation). Chunked prefill for ultra-long contexts. Config: `--max-model-len 128000` for 128K context models.

**Best For**: Enterprise deployments with >10 concurrent users, organizations replacing OpenAI API with full compatibility, GPU-rich environments (data center, cloud), high-throughput batch inference pipelines, multi-tenant LLM platforms, organizations with MLOps teams.

**Worst For**: Apple Silicon (no Metal support), CPU-only deployments, GGUF model format (convert to HuggingFace format instead), single-user or low-concurrency scenarios (overkill, Ollama is simpler), developers without Python/ML background.

**Production Launch Command**:
```bash
vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --port 8000 \
  --tensor-parallel-size 2 \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
  --max-model-len 32768 \
  --enable-chunked-prefill \
  --api-key your-secret-key
```

title: text-generation-webui (oobabooga): Maximum Model Format Flexibility
content:
text-generation-webui, developed by oobabooga, is the most feature-rich and model-format-flexible local LLM interface available. It functions as both a web UI and an API server, supporting virtually every inference backend through a modular extension system.

**Installation Complexity: 3/5 (moderate)**
One-click installers available for Windows, Linux, macOS via GitHub releases. Manual install: `git clone`, run `start_linux.sh` (or Windows/macOS equivalent) which creates a conda environment and installs dependencies. Slower to install than Ollama due to the full Python/conda environment setup (~5-15 minutes). Docker available.

**Supported Model Formats**: The broadest format support available: GGUF (via llama.cpp backend), GPTQ (via AutoGPTQ), AWQ (via AutoAWQ), EXL2/ExLlamaV2 (highest GPU performance for quantized models), HuggingFace Transformers (FP16/BF16), GGML (legacy), RWKV, Mamba. The modular backend system means new formats are added quickly by the community.

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Complete via the 'openai' extension. Must be explicitly enabled: `--extensions openai`. Implements /v1/chat/completions, /v1/completions, /v1/embeddings. Binds to port 5000 by default. The extension is actively maintained and closely tracks the OpenAI spec.

**Performance**: Highly variable by backend selected:
- ExLlamaV2 backend, RTX 4090, 13B GPTQ: 90-130 t/s (peak GPU quantized performance)
- llama.cpp backend, RTX 4090, 13B GGUF Q4: 80-110 t/s
- HuggingFace Transformers (FP16), RTX 4090, 7B: 40-70 t/s
- CPU via llama.cpp, 7B Q4: 7-15 t/s

**GPU Support**: CUDA (all backends), ROCm/HIP (AMD via ExLlamaV2 and llama.cpp), Metal (macOS via llama.cpp backend), Vulkan (llama.cpp backend).

**Multi-Model Serving**: Single active model at a time. Switching models requires loading a new model through the UI or API. Not designed for simultaneous multi-model serving.

**Context Window**: Depends on backend. ExLlamaV2 supports very long contexts (64K+) efficiently. llama.cpp backend same as standalone llama.cpp.

**Best For**: Researchers and power users who need to switch between many backends and formats, organizations evaluating model performance across quantization levels (easy A/B comparison), users who need ExLlamaV2 for maximum GPU quantized performance, fine-tuned model testing (LoRA, full fine-tunes in any format), developers who need the training/fine-tuning UI (Trainer extension).

**Worst For**: Production API serving (Ollama or vLLM are more stable), Docker-first environments (GUI-heavy), beginners who find the UI overwhelming, Apple Silicon users (CUDA-optimized backends don't apply), performance-consistency requirements (backend variability).

**API Enable Command**:
```bash
python server.py \
  --model llama-3.1-8b-instruct \
  --extensions openai \
  --listen \
  --api-port 5000 \
  --loader exllamav2 \
  --gpu-split 23
```

title: GPT4All: Simplest Privacy-First Local LLM for Non-Technical Users
content:
GPT4All by Nomic AI targets the widest possible audience with the simplest possible installation. It is the right choice when the user is non-technical, privacy is the primary concern, and internet connectivity cannot be guaranteed.

**Installation Complexity: 1/5 (trivial)**
Download a single executable from gpt4all.io. No Python, no conda, no Docker, no command line. Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux. Self-contained binary. Model downloads happen within the app.

**Supported Model Formats**: GGUF (primary), Nomic's own fine-tuned models distributed through the Nomic model portal. Limited to models available in the GPT4All model library (curated selection vs. full HuggingFace access).

**OpenAI API Compatibility**: Partial and limited. The Local API Server (must be enabled in settings) implements /v1/models, /v1/completions, and /v1/chat/completions only. It binds exclusively to localhost (127.0.0.1) by design — you CANNOT expose it to other machines on the network. Not a suitable drop-in replacement for production OpenAI integrations.

**Performance**: Competitive with Ollama for GGUF models on similar hardware (same underlying llama.cpp engine):
- Apple M2, 7B Q4: 30-45 t/s
- RTX 3090, 7B Q4: 70-100 t/s
- CPU-only, 7B Q4: 5-10 t/s

**GPU Support**: CUDA (NVIDIA), Metal (Apple Silicon via llama.cpp), Vulkan (some AMD support), CPU fallback.

**Multi-Model Serving**: Single model at a time, managed through the UI.

**Context Window**: Limited to what llama.cpp supports, configurable in settings.

**Best For**: Non-technical end users who need a completely offline, private AI assistant, organizations deploying AI to employees who should not need to use a command line, GDPR/HIPAA scenarios where zero data leaves the machine, small businesses with no IT staff, educational environments (simple to deploy, hard to misconfigure).

**Worst For**: API integrations (localhost-only server, partial API), network-accessible deployments, model diversity (curated selection only), enterprise authentication, high-concurrency API serving, Docker/server environments.

**2025 Update**: GPT4All added 'Remote Models' as a first-class feature in 2025, allowing users to connect to OpenAI, Groq, and Mistral alongside local models from the same interface. This makes it a viable unified interface for organizations using both cloud and local AI.

title: Local LLM Server Decision Tree: Which Server Should You Choose?
content:
Use this decision tree to select the right local LLM server for your specific context. Start at Question 1 and follow the branches.

**Q1: Is this a desktop application for individuals or a server deployment for applications?**
- Desktop/individual → Go to Q2
- Server/application integration → Go to Q4

**Q2 (Desktop path): Do you need a visual interface and ease of use over performance?**
- Yes, I want maximum simplicity → **GPT4All** (non-technical users, fully offline, zero config)
- Yes, but I want model variety and team features → **LM Studio** (best GUI, HuggingFace browser, MLX for Mac)
- Yes, but I need agent/MCP support → **Jan.ai** (agentic workspaces, MCP integration, ChatGPT replacement)
- No, I'm comfortable with CLI but want a local API → Go to Q4

**Q3: What hardware are you using?**
- Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) → **LM Studio** (MLX support) or **Ollama** (best Metal optimization)
- NVIDIA GPU → **LM Studio** or **Ollama** for desktop
- AMD GPU → **LM Studio** (Vulkan support) for desktop
- CPU-only → **GPT4All** or **LM Studio** (Vulkan for iGPU)

**Q4 (Server/API path): Do you need NVIDIA GPU-only or high concurrency (>10 concurrent users)?**
- Yes, high concurrency + NVIDIA GPU → **vLLM** (PagedAttention, 2-24x higher throughput)
- No, mixed hardware or moderate concurrency → Go to Q5

**Q5: Do you need an OpenAI drop-in replacement with minimal configuration?**
- Yes → **Ollama** (best developer experience, lowest config, Docker-native)
- I need multi-modal (images + audio + text) → **LocalAI** (unified OpenAI-compatible stack)
- I need maximum model format support (GPTQ, AWQ, EXL2) → **text-generation-webui** with ExLlamaV2 backend
- I want the lowest possible overhead and maximum control → **llama.cpp server** (raw engine, no abstraction)

**Q6: Do you need Docker/Kubernetes?**
- Yes → **Ollama** (official Docker image, Helm chart, well-documented K8s patterns) or **LocalAI** (Docker-first design) or **vLLM** (Docker + K8s operator)
- No → Any of the above work; Ollama is still simplest

**Q7: What model formats do you have?**
- GGUF files → Any server works; Ollama is easiest
- GPTQ / AWQ (GPU quantized) → **vLLM**, **text-generation-webui**, or **LocalAI** (exllama backend)
- HuggingFace SafeTensors → **vLLM** (native), **text-generation-webui** (transformers backend)
- Mixed formats → **text-generation-webui** (broadest format support)

**Quick Reference Matrix**:
| Server | Install | OpenAI Compat | GPU Support | Concurrency | Best Use Case |
|--------|---------|---------------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| Ollama | 1/5 | 95% | CUDA+Metal+ROCm | Medium | Default API server |
| LocalAI | 3/5 | 99% | CUDA+Metal | Medium | Multi-modal stack |
| LM Studio | 1/5 | 95% | CUDA+Metal+Vulkan | Low | Desktop teams |
| Jan.ai | 1/5 | 90% | CUDA+Metal | Low | Agent workspaces |
| llama.cpp | 4/5 | 80% | All backends | Low | Max performance |
| vLLM | 3/5 | 100% | CUDA+ROCm | Very High | Enterprise prod |
| oobabooga | 3/5 | 85% | CUDA+Metal | Low | Format flexibility |
| GPT4All | 1/5 | 40% | CUDA+Metal | None | Non-technical users |

title: Memory Requirements by Model Size and Quantization Level
content:
Understanding memory requirements is the most critical hardware planning step for private AI deployment. The formula is: model_size_GB ≈ (parameters × bytes_per_parameter) + KV_cache. Quantization dramatically reduces bytes per parameter.

**Bytes per parameter by format**:
- F32 (full precision): 4 bytes/param
- F16 / BF16: 2 bytes/param
- Q8_0 (8-bit quantization): ~1 byte/param + overhead
- Q5_K_M (5-bit K-quant): ~0.67 bytes/param
- Q4_K_M (4-bit K-quant, recommended default): ~0.5 bytes/param
- Q3_K_M (3-bit): ~0.38 bytes/param
- Q2_K (2-bit, low quality): ~0.25 bytes/param

**Memory requirements table (GGUF VRAM/RAM needed, model only)**:

3B parameters:
- Q4_K_M: ~2.0 GB — runs on anything, even 4GB iGPU
- Q5_K_M: ~2.5 GB
- Q8_0: ~3.3 GB
- F16: ~6.0 GB

7B parameters:
- Q4_K_M: ~4.1 GB — fits in 6GB VRAM (RTX 3060, older cards)
- Q5_K_M: ~5.0 GB
- Q8_0: ~7.7 GB — requires 8GB VRAM (RTX 3060 12GB, RTX 3070)
- F16: ~14.0 GB — requires 16GB VRAM (RTX 4080)

13B parameters:
- Q4_K_M: ~7.3 GB — fits in 8GB VRAM with room for KV cache
- Q5_K_M: ~9.0 GB — requires 12GB VRAM
- Q8_0: ~13.5 GB — requires 16GB VRAM
- F16: ~26.0 GB — requires RTX 3090 (24GB, tight) or A5000 (24GB)

34B parameters:
- Q4_K_M: ~19.0 GB — requires RTX 3090/4090 (24GB)
- Q5_K_M: ~23.0 GB — tight on 24GB
- Q8_0: ~35.0 GB — requires dual GPU or A100 40GB
- F16: ~68.0 GB — requires A100 80GB or H100

70B parameters:
- Q4_K_M: ~38.0 GB — requires dual RTX 3090/4090 or A100 40GB
- Q5_K_M: ~47.0 GB — requires A100 80GB
- Q8_0: ~70.0 GB — requires H100 80GB or dual A100 80GB
- F16: ~140.0 GB — requires 2x H100 80GB

**KV Cache Additional Memory** (add to model size):
KV cache size = batch_size × sequence_length × n_layers × n_heads × head_dim × 2 × sizeof(float16)
For a 7B Llama model at 4096 context, 1 sequence: ~512 MB additional
For 32K context, 1 sequence: ~4 GB additional
For 128K context, 1 sequence: ~16 GB additional

**CPU RAM vs GPU VRAM**:
On CPU-only inference, use system RAM instead of VRAM. Rule of thumb: have 2x the model size in RAM (model + working memory). For a 7B Q4_K_M (4.1GB model), use a system with 16GB RAM minimum, 32GB recommended.

**Apple Silicon unified memory advantage**: Apple Silicon shares memory between CPU and GPU. An M3 Max with 36GB unified memory can run a 13B Q8 model that would not fit on a 16GB discrete GPU, because the entire memory pool is available to the neural engine.

title: CPU-Only Deployment: Sizing for Small Business to Enterprise
content:
CPU-only deployment is viable for private AI when GPU procurement is not feasible, when energy efficiency matters more than speed, or when running smaller models at moderate throughput. Modern CPUs with AVX-512 and Intel AMX make CPU inference more competitive than in earlier years.

**Tier 1 — Minimum Viable (Small Business, 1-3 Users)**
Target: 7B models at Q4_K_M, 5-15 tokens/second, single-user experience

Minimum specs:
- CPU: Any modern AMD Ryzen 5 7600 or Intel Core i5-12th gen (AVX2 required, AVX-512 beneficial)
- RAM: 16GB DDR5 (8GB for model, 8GB for OS/applications)
- Storage: 256GB NVMe SSD (fast model loading, NVMe vs SATA matters for 4-8GB files)
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu 22.04 recommended for llama.cpp/Ollama CPU performance)

Expected performance:
- Ollama, Llama-3.2-3B Q4_K_M: 20-35 t/s
- Ollama, Llama-3.1-8B Q4_K_M: 8-15 t/s

**Tier 2 — Standard Small Business (5-15 Users, Shared)**
Target: 13B models at Q4_K_M, single-user requests queued

Recommended specs:
- CPU: AMD Ryzen 9 7950X (16 cores, AVX-512) or Intel Core i9-13900K (24 cores, AVX-512)
- RAM: 64GB DDR5 (model + working memory + OS overhead)
- Storage: 1TB NVMe Gen4 SSD
- Cooling: High-performance air cooling or 240mm AIO (CPU sustains 100% load during inference)

Expected performance:
- Llama-3.1-8B Q4_K_M: 15-25 t/s
- Mistral-7B Q5_K_M: 12-20 t/s
- Codestral-22B Q4: 5-10 t/s

**Tier 3 — Standard Enterprise CPU Server (Department-Level)**
Target: 70B models at Q4_K_M (with CPU spread), or 13-34B at full speed

Recommended specs:
- CPU: Dual Intel Xeon Scalable 4th gen (Sapphire Rapids, AMX-enabled) or AMD EPYC Genoa
- RAM: 256GB-512GB DDR5 ECC (enables 70B Q4 fully in RAM)
- Storage: 2TB+ NVMe
- Network: 25GbE (for team access to inference server)

Expected performance with Intel AMX:
- 7B Q4_K_M with AMX: 50-100 t/s (dramatic improvement vs non-AMX)
- 13B Q4_K_M: 25-50 t/s
- 70B Q4_K_M (loaded in 256GB RAM): 5-12 t/s

**Intel vs AMD for CPU inference (2025-2026)**:
- Intel Xeon AMX advantage: AMX (Advanced Matrix Extensions) in Sapphire/Granite Rapids achieves 5.4 TFLOPS peak on matrix math vs 1.8 TFLOPS for AVX-512, delivering ~2x faster inference on quantized models. AMX is integrated into PyTorch (via oneDNN) and OpenVINO.
- AMD EPYC AVX-512 advantage: AMD Zen 4/5 AVX-512 with VNNI+BF16 provides competitive performance. In some benchmarks, AMD EPYC 9555 outperforms Intel Xeon 6767P by ~1.4x on Llama-3.1-8B inference throughput at high concurrency.
- Practical recommendation: Intel AMX-enabled Xeon for single-node inference workloads; AMD EPYC for memory-bandwidth-heavy multi-request scenarios or when per-core price is critical.

**Apple Silicon CPU Tiers (Unified Memory)**:
- M3 (base, 8-24GB): 7B Q4_K_M at 35-50 t/s. Best for: individual developer workstation.
- M3 Pro (18-36GB): 13B Q4_K_M at 30-45 t/s. Best for: developer or small team server.
- M3 Max (36-128GB): 34B Q4_K_M at 25-40 t/s. Best for: team inference server, fits large models.
- M3 Ultra (192GB): 70B Q8_0 fits in unified memory. 70B Q4_K_M at 30-45 t/s. Best for: department-level on-prem server.
- M4 Max (128GB): ~20-25% faster than M3 Max. 70B Q4_K_M at 40-55 t/s estimated.
- Mac Studio/Mac Pro as inference server: Connect via Ollama, serve team over LAN. Energy efficient (40-80W vs 450W for RTX 4090 system).

title: GPU-Accelerated Deployment: Consumer to Enterprise Hardware Guide
content:
GPU acceleration provides 5-30x better inference performance than CPU for models that fit in VRAM. This guide covers the full spectrum from consumer cards to enterprise server GPUs.

**Consumer Tier — Individual Developer / Small Team**

NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24GB GDDR6X, $1,599 MSRP):
- The gold standard for local LLM inference in 2025.
- VRAM: 24GB fits 7B (F16), 13B (Q8), 34B (Q4) all comfortably.
- Performance: 120-175 t/s for 7B Q4_K_M, 65-100 t/s for 13B Q4_K_M.
- 70B Q4_K_M: too large for single 24GB card (38GB needed). Use CPU offload or quantize to Q2_K (~17GB).
- Power: 450W TDP. Requires 850W+ PSU.
- Best for: Single developer running models up to 34B, or small team with 2-5 users.

NVIDIA RTX 3090 (24GB GDDR6X, ~$700-900 used):
- Same 24GB VRAM as 4090. About 30-40% slower per-dollar compared to 4090.
- Performance: 80-110 t/s for 7B Q4. Good value for budget-conscious deployments.
- Can run 70B Q4_K_M split across two 3090s (dual-GPU, tensor parallel).

NVIDIA RTX 4080 Super (16GB, ~$999):
- Fits 7B (F16 barely), 13B (Q4/Q5). Can't fit 34B Q4.
- 100-130 t/s for 7B Q4. Budget consumer option with meaningful capability limit.

**Workstation Tier — Department / Team of 10-30**

NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation (48GB GDDR6, ~$6,800):
- 48GB VRAM fits 70B Q4_K_M on a single card (38GB model + KV cache).
- Performance: 80-120 t/s for 70B Q4_K_M. Enables full 70B serving for a small team.
- ECC memory option for data integrity. Workstation form factor (PCIe, no SXM).
- Best for: Department AI server handling 70B models for 5-20 concurrent users with Ollama or vLLM.

AMD Radeon Pro W7900 (48GB):
- Alternative to RTX 6000 Ada at similar price.
- ROCm support for PyTorch/vLLM (experimental but improving rapidly in 2025).
- Lower inference performance than NVIDIA equivalent (~60-70% of RTX 6000 Ada speed).

**Server Tier — Enterprise**

NVIDIA A100 80GB SXM (data center, ~$10,000-15,000 used, $25,000+ new):
- 80GB HBM2e, 2,000 GB/s memory bandwidth (vs 1,018 GB/s for RTX 4090).
- Performance: 138 t/s on 70B at FP8, ~80 t/s at BF16. Handles 70B with large batches.
- NVLink for multi-GPU: 4x A100s = 320GB pool, serves 405B models.
- Best for: Enterprise inference cluster, multi-user platform for 50+ users.

NVIDIA H100 80GB SXM (data center, $25,000-35,000):
- 80GB HBM3, 3,350 GB/s memory bandwidth. The fastest available as of 2025.
- Performance: 144 t/s per H100 on 70B. Up to 30x better throughput than A100 for certain workloads.
- Transformer Engine (FP8 training and inference).
- Best for: High-scale LLM platforms, organizations with hundreds of concurrent users.

**Multi-GPU Patterns**:
- Tensor Parallelism: Model layers split across GPUs (requires NVLink or high-bandwidth interconnect). Use with vLLM `--tensor-parallel-size N`.
- Pipeline Parallelism: Model stages across GPUs (tolerates PCIe bandwidth, higher latency).
- Data Parallelism: Same model on each GPU, load-balanced (best for high-concurrency serving).
- Dual RTX 4090 via PCIe 4.0: 48GB total, runs 70B Q4_K_M split. ~100-130 t/s combined on 70B. Requires llama.cpp `--split-mode row` or vLLM tensor parallel.

**GPU Memory Bandwidth vs VRAM**:
Inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound, for autoregressive generation. This is why A100/H100 with HBM are faster despite similar CUDA core counts to RTX 4090: HBM bandwidth (2,000+ GB/s) vs GDDR6X (1,018 GB/s).

title: Tokens Per Second Benchmark Reference by Hardware Class
content:
Realistic tokens-per-second benchmarks for planning private AI deployment capacity. All figures use Llama-family models unless noted, measured with Ollama or llama.cpp (comparable for single-request latency) or vLLM (for concurrent throughput).

**Important caveat**: Tokens/sec varies with: prompt length (prefill is faster than generation), context window used, quantization format, concurrent request count, and thermal throttling. Numbers below represent sustained single-request generation speed.

**3B Parameter Models (Q4_K_M)**:
- Mobile CPU (M2, base): 60-80 t/s
- M3 Pro: 80-100 t/s
- M3 Max: 120-150 t/s
- Ryzen 9 7950X (CPU-only): 25-40 t/s
- Intel Xeon AMX: 40-60 t/s
- RTX 4090: 200-300 t/s (GPU overkill for 3B)

**7B Parameter Models (Q4_K_M)**:
- CPU: Ryzen 9 7950X: 15-22 t/s
- CPU: Intel Xeon (AMX-enabled): 35-55 t/s
- CPU: AMD EPYC 9654 (dual socket): 25-40 t/s
- Apple M2: 30-45 t/s
- Apple M3 Pro (18GB): 45-60 t/s
- Apple M3 Max (36GB): 70-90 t/s
- Apple M3 Ultra (192GB): 90-110 t/s
- Apple M4 Max: 90-110 t/s (est.)
- NVIDIA RTX 3090 (24GB): 80-110 t/s
- NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24GB): 120-160 t/s
- NVIDIA RTX 5090 (32GB): 200-250 t/s
- NVIDIA A100 (40GB): 110-140 t/s
- NVIDIA H100 (80GB): 140-180 t/s

**13B Parameter Models (Q4_K_M)**:
- CPU: Ryzen 9 7950X: 8-14 t/s
- CPU: Intel Xeon AMX: 18-30 t/s
- Apple M3 Pro (18GB): 25-38 t/s
- Apple M3 Max (36GB): 40-55 t/s
- Apple M3 Ultra (192GB): 55-75 t/s
- NVIDIA RTX 3090: 55-75 t/s
- NVIDIA RTX 4090: 65-95 t/s
- NVIDIA A100 80GB: 100-130 t/s
- NVIDIA H100 80GB: 120-160 t/s

**34B Parameter Models (Q4_K_M)**:
- CPU (256GB RAM server): 4-8 t/s
- Apple M3 Ultra (192GB): 35-50 t/s
- Apple M4 Max (128GB): 40-55 t/s (fits at Q4)
- NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada (48GB): 50-75 t/s
- Dual RTX 4090 (48GB total): 45-70 t/s
- NVIDIA A100 80GB: 80-110 t/s
- NVIDIA H100 80GB: 100-140 t/s

**70B Parameter Models (Q4_K_M, ~38GB model size)**:
- CPU (512GB RAM, Xeon AMX): 3-7 t/s
- Apple M3 Ultra (192GB): 25-40 t/s
- Apple M4 Max (128GB): DOES NOT FIT (need 128GB+ for 70B Q4, Apple M4 Ultra fits)
- NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada (48GB): 35-55 t/s
- Dual RTX 4090 (48GB, PCIe): 30-50 t/s (PCIe bandwidth limits tensor parallel)
- NVIDIA A100 80GB: 60-90 t/s
- NVIDIA H100 80GB: 80-120 t/s
- Dual A100 80GB (NVLink): 120-180 t/s

**Concurrent Throughput (vLLM, 20 concurrent users)**:
- RTX 4090, 7B FP16: 600-900 total t/s
- A100 80GB, 70B INT8: 300-500 total t/s
- H100 80GB, 70B FP8: 800-1200 total t/s

**Rule of Thumb for Capacity Planning**:
- 1 active user needs: model_tokens_per_second / average_response_length_tokens = requests_per_minute
- For 100 t/s and 300-token average responses: ~20 requests/minute capacity
- Add 3x headroom for burst traffic

title: Docker Container Sizing and Kubernetes Deployment for Ollama
content:
Running Ollama in Docker and Kubernetes requires careful resource configuration to avoid OOM kills, GPU scheduling conflicts, and model loading failures.

**Docker Single Container (Production-Grade)**

Basic GPU-enabled Ollama container:
```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    runtime: nvidia  # Requires nvidia-container-runtime
    ports:
      - '11434:11434'
    environment:
      - OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=2
      - OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4
      - OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=30m
      - OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434
    volumes:
      - ollama-models:/root/.ollama
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]
        limits:
          memory: 32G  # Accommodate model + KV cache + OS
    restart: unless-stopped
```

**Memory Sizing Rules for Docker**:
- VRAM limit: controlled by `nvidia-smi` device assignment, not Docker memory limits
- Container memory limit = GPU model size + CPU RAM overhead + OS (typically 4-8GB OS overhead)
- 7B Q4 container: memory limit 16G minimum, 24G recommended
- 13B Q4 container: memory limit 24G minimum
- 70B Q4 (A100): memory limit 64G
- Add 50% headroom for KV cache growth with long conversations

**CPU-Only Docker Container**:
```yaml
services:
  ollama-cpu:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports:
      - '11434:11434'
    environment:
      - OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=2  # Limit parallelism on CPU
    volumes:
      - ollama-models:/root/.ollama
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '8'          # 8 of 16 total cores for inference
          memory: 32G
        reservations:
          cpus: '4'
          memory: 16G
```

**Kubernetes Deployment Patterns**

Per-GPU Pod (most common):
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ollama
spec:
  replicas: 1  # One replica per GPU node
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ollama
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: ollama
        image: ollama/ollama:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 11434
        env:
        - name: OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS
          value: '2'
        - name: OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL
          value: '4'
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: 16Gi
            nvidia.com/gpu: '1'
          limits:
            memory: 32Gi
            nvidia.com/gpu: '1'
        volumeMounts:
        - name: model-storage
          mountPath: /root/.ollama
      volumes:
      - name: model-storage
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: ollama-models-pvc
      nodeSelector:
        accelerator: nvidia-rtx4090
```

**Horizontal Scaling Strategy**:
Ollama instances are stateless in terms of request routing (models stored in shared PVC). Scale horizontally by:
1. Multiple Ollama pods behind a Kubernetes Service (round-robin is sufficient for most cases)
2. Use nginx or Traefik as load balancer with sticky sessions for model locality
3. Pre-warm models on startup using an initContainer that runs `ollama pull <model>`

**GPU Time-Slicing for Multiple Pods on One GPU**:
```yaml
# ConfigMap for NVIDIA device plugin
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: time-slicing-config
data:
  config.json: |
    {
      "version": "v1",
      "flags": {"migStrategy": "none"},
      "sharing": {
        "timeSlicing": {
          "resources": [{"name": "nvidia.com/gpu", "replicas": 4}]
        }
      }
    }
```
Note: Time-slicing increases context switching overhead. Only use for workloads with low concurrent utilization.

**Model Loading Time**: Expect 5-30 seconds for model loading on pod start. Configure readinessProbe with initialDelaySeconds: 30 and use preloading to minimize cold start.

**Persistent Volume Recommendation**: Use ReadWriteMany (NFS or Ceph RBD) for shared model storage, or ReadWriteOnce with DaemonSet deployment (one pod per GPU node).

title: vLLM Docker and Kubernetes Production Deployment
content:
vLLM is designed for production serving and has mature Docker and Kubernetes deployment patterns. It is the recommended choice when you have NVIDIA GPUs and need to serve >10 concurrent users reliably.

**Docker Quick Start**:
```bash
# Single GPU, single model
docker run --runtime nvidia --gpus all \
  -v ~/.cache/huggingface:/root/.cache/huggingface \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  --env HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=hf_... \
  vllm/vllm-openai:latest \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --max-model-len 32768
```

**Docker Compose (Production)**:
```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  vllm:
    image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
    runtime: nvidia
    ports:
      - '8000:8000'
    volumes:
      - huggingface-cache:/root/.cache/huggingface
      - ./models:/models  # Local model files
    environment:
      - HUGGING_FACE_HUB_TOKEN=${HF_TOKEN}
    command: >
      --model /models/llama-3.1-8b-instruct
      --tensor-parallel-size 2
      --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90
      --max-model-len 32768
      --enable-chunked-prefill
      --api-key ${VLLM_API_KEY}
      --port 8000
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 2  # Match tensor-parallel-size
              capabilities: [gpu]
        limits:
          memory: 64G
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD', 'curl', '-f', 'http://localhost:8000/health']
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
```

**Kubernetes Deployment (Multi-GPU)**:
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vllm-server
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: vllm
        image: vllm/vllm-openai:latest
        command:
        - python3
        - -m
        - vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server
        args:
        - --model=/models/llama-3.1-70b-instruct
        - --tensor-parallel-size=4
        - --gpu-memory-utilization=0.95
        - --max-model-len=32768
        - --enable-chunked-prefill
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: 256Gi
            nvidia.com/gpu: '4'
          limits:
            memory: 256Gi
            nvidia.com/gpu: '4'
        volumeMounts:
        - name: model-storage
          mountPath: /models
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 120  # Model loading takes time
          periodSeconds: 10
```

**Sizing Guidelines for vLLM**:
- Container memory = GPU VRAM × number_of_GPUs + 16GB system overhead
- gpu-memory-utilization=0.90: Reserve 10% for CUDA overhead and spikes
- tensor-parallel-size must equal the number of GPUs assigned
- Max concurrent requests: roughly GPU_VRAM_GB / (avg_sequence_length × KV_cache_per_token)

**Load Balancing Multiple vLLM Instances**:
For models that fit on one GPU, run multiple instances behind nginx:
```nginx
upstream vllm_backend {
    least_conn;
    server vllm-1:8000;
    server vllm-2:8000;
    server vllm-3:8000;
}
```
This provides true horizontal scaling with stateless routing.

**Key Production Environment Variables**:
```bash
VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD=spawn
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1,2,3
NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL=WARNING  # Reduce log noise in production
```

title: Apple Silicon as a Private AI Server: Mac Studio and Mac Pro Guide
content:
Apple Silicon Macs are uniquely well-suited as private AI servers for small to medium teams due to their combination of large unified memory, energy efficiency, and silent operation. This guide covers the practical deployment of Apple Silicon as an inference server.

**Why Apple Silicon is Different**
Traditional inference runs on discrete GPUs with separate VRAM. Apple Silicon uses unified memory — the same physical memory pool is shared between CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine. This eliminates the VRAM bottleneck that limits discrete GPU deployments.

Key advantage: An M3 Ultra with 192GB unified memory can run a 70B model at Q8_0 quantization (requiring ~72GB) — something no single discrete GPU can match without going to A100 80GB tier.

**Model Matrix for Apple Silicon (2025-2026)**:

M3 (8-24GB unified memory):
- Fits comfortably: 7B Q4_K_M (4.1GB), 3B Q8 (3.3GB)
- Fits tight: 7B Q8 (7.7GB on 8GB config = NO, 24GB config = yes)
- Performance: 45-65 t/s on 7B Q4

M3 Pro (18-36GB):
- Fits: 13B Q4_K_M (7.3GB), 13B Q8 (13.5GB on 36GB config)
- Performance: 45-60 t/s on 7B, 30-45 t/s on 13B Q4

M3 Max (36-128GB):
- Fits: 34B Q4_K_M (19GB), 34B Q8 (35GB on 64GB+ config)
- Performance: 70-90 t/s on 7B, 40-55 t/s on 13B, 25-40 t/s on 34B Q4

M3 Ultra (192GB):
- Fits: 70B Q8_0 (70GB), 70B F16 (140GB on 192GB config)
- Performance: 30-45 t/s on 70B Q4_K_M
- Best Apple Silicon option for 70B models

M4 Max (128GB, ~2025-2026):
- ~25% faster than M3 Max per Apple benchmarks
- 153 GB/s memory bandwidth (vs 120 GB/s M3 Max) = faster for inference
- Fits 70B Q4_K_M (38GB) comfortably in 128GB config
- Performance: 40-55 t/s on 70B Q4, 90-110 t/s on 7B Q4

**Server Setup (Mac Studio / Mac Pro)**:
```bash
# Install Ollama (best Metal support)
brew install ollama

# Start as persistent service
brew services start ollama

# Configure for network access
export OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434  # Add to /etc/launchd.conf or plist

# Pull models
ollama pull llama3.1:70b-instruct-q4_K_M

# Verify GPU layers (should show all layers on GPU)
ollama run llama3.1:70b -- /verbose
```

**Ollama Launchd Plist for Network Access** (required to expose beyond localhost):
```xml
<!-- /Library/LaunchDaemons/com.ollama.server.plist -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN"...>
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>Label</key><string>com.ollama.server</string>
  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array><string>/opt/homebrew/bin/ollama</string><string>serve</string></array>
  <key>EnvironmentVariables</key>
  <dict>
    <key>OLLAMA_HOST</key><string>0.0.0.0:11434</string>
    <key>OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS</key><string>2</string>
  </dict>
  <key>RunAtLoad</key><true/>
  <key>KeepAlive</key><true/>
</dict>
</plist>
```

**Energy Efficiency Comparison**:
- M3 Max under full LLM inference load: 40-80W total system power
- RTX 4090 system under full load: 450-600W total system power
- For 24/7 operation: Apple Silicon costs ~$35-70/month in electricity (at $0.12/kWh), vs $400-650/month for RTX 4090 system

**Limitation**: No CUDA = no vLLM, no Flash Attention GPU acceleration. Use Ollama or llama.cpp with Metal. For multi-user concurrency, Ollama with OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=4-8 provides reasonable scaling for teams of 5-15 users on an M3/M4 Ultra.

title: Private AI Deployment Architecture Patterns: Three Reference Designs
content:
Three proven architecture patterns cover the majority of private AI deployment scenarios. Select based on team size, budget, and GPU availability.

**Pattern 1: Single-Server Mac Studio (Small Team, 5-15 Users)**

Components:
- Hardware: Mac Studio M3 Ultra (192GB) or M4 Max (128GB)
- Software: Ollama + nginx reverse proxy
- Network: Wired Ethernet on internal LAN
- Model: Llama-3.1-70B-Q4_K_M (for M3 Ultra) or Llama-3.1-34B-Q4_K_M (for M4 Max 128GB)

Architecture:
```
[Users] → [nginx :443 TLS] → [Ollama :11434] → [Mac Studio (M3 Ultra)]
```

nginx config for Ollama:
```nginx
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/certs/ai.company.com.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/private/ai.company.com.key;
    
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:11434;
        proxy_set_header Authorization 'Bearer $http_authorization';
        proxy_read_timeout 300s;  # Long timeout for slow inference
        proxy_buffering off;      # Required for streaming responses
    }
}
```

Capacity: 5-15 users with queued requests. Peak: ~4 concurrent sessions at 40 t/s each = 160 t/s total. This handles a team with typical usage patterns (not all users generating simultaneously).

Cost: ~$5,000-8,000 hardware + $35/month electricity. Amortized over 3 years: ~$150/month total.

**Pattern 2: GPU Server with vLLM (Department, 20-100 Users)**

Components:
- Hardware: Workstation with 2× NVIDIA RTX 4090 or 1× RTX 6000 Ada
- OS: Ubuntu 22.04 Server
- Software: vLLM + Docker + nginx
- Model: Llama-3.1-70B at Q4_K_M (dual 4090) or BF16 (RTX 6000 Ada 48GB)

Architecture:
```
[Users] → [nginx load balancer] → [vLLM instance 1: port 8000]
                                  [vLLM instance 2: port 8001]  (optional replica)
[Admin] → [Grafana/Prometheus monitoring]
```

Capacity: 20-50 concurrent users. vLLM's PagedAttention handles burst traffic. Prometheus metrics at /metrics for monitoring queue depth and latency.

Cost: ~$8,000-15,000 hardware + $450/month electricity for 24/7 operation.

**Pattern 3: Kubernetes Cluster (Enterprise, 100+ Users)**

Components:
- Hardware: 4-8 NVIDIA A100 80GB or H100 nodes
- Orchestration: Kubernetes + NVIDIA GPU Operator
- Software: vLLM with tensor parallelism, Prometheus, Grafana
- Models: Multiple models (small for quick responses, large for complex tasks)
- Auth: OAuth2 proxy in front of vLLM for SSO integration

Architecture:
```
[Users/Apps] → [Auth Proxy (Keycloak/Okta)] 
             → [Kong API Gateway (rate limiting, usage tracking)]
             → [vLLM-small (7B, 4 pods)] for quick requests
             → [vLLM-large (70B, 2 pods)] for complex requests
             → [Model Router (RouteLLM)] for intelligent routing
```

Capacity: 100-500 users. Auto-scaling via Kubernetes HPA based on queue depth. Multiple model tiers allow routing simple queries to fast/cheap models and complex queries to large models.

Capacity planning: H100 serves 70B at ~100-120 t/s per instance. 2 H100s × 100 t/s × 2 instances = 400 total t/s. At 200 tokens/response average and 60-second response windows: ~120 requests/minute capacity.

**Common to All Patterns**:
- TLS termination at nginx/gateway (never expose raw Ollama/vLLM without auth)
- API key authentication minimum, OAuth2 for enterprise
- Prometheus + Grafana for monitoring tokens/sec, request latency, queue depth
- Automated model updates via CI/CD (pull new models, verify, hot-swap)
- Backup: model files on NAS or object storage (models are large, 4-140GB each)

title: Model Format Selection Guide: GGUF vs GPTQ vs AWQ vs SafeTensors
content:
Choosing the right model format is a critical deployment decision that affects which servers you can use, performance, and storage requirements.

**GGUF (GPT-Unified Format)**
The format for local deployment in 2025-2026. Developed by llama.cpp, adopted as the de facto standard.

Characteristics:
- Single file format (one .gguf file per model, vs many shards)
- Supports all quantization levels: Q2_K through Q8_0, F16, BF16
- Cross-platform: runs on CUDA, Metal, Vulkan, ROCm, CPU
- The K-quant variants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) use mixed precision for better quality vs size tradeoff
- Supported by: Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio, Jan.ai, LocalAI (llama.cpp backend), text-generation-webui (llama.cpp backend)

When to use: CPU inference, Apple Silicon, any local deployment where you want one format that runs everywhere. Default choice.

Q4_K_M is the recommended default — it provides ~4x size reduction from F16 with typically <1% quality loss on most benchmarks.

**GPTQ (GPT Quantization)**
GPU-native quantization using group-wise quantization. Best for NVIDIA GPU inference.

Characteristics:
- Requires NVIDIA CUDA for inference (no CPU/Metal)
- Typically 4-bit, sometimes 8-bit
- Better quality than GGUF Q4 at similar size in some benchmarks
- AutoGPTQ is the primary library
- Supported by: vLLM, text-generation-webui (ExLlamaV2 backend), LocalAI (exllama backend)

When to use: NVIDIA GPU-only environments where you've evaluated GPTQ models outperform GGUF for your specific use case. Less common in 2025-2026 as GGUF K-quants have caught up in quality.

**AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization)**
More sophisticated 4-bit quantization that activates only the most important weights at full precision.

Characteristics:
- Typically better quality than GPTQ at 4-bit
- Requires CUDA (NVIDIA GPU)
- AutoAWQ library for quantization and inference
- Supported by: vLLM (native), text-generation-webui, LocalAI

When to use: Production NVIDIA GPU serving where model quality is paramount and you have time to evaluate. AWQ generally outperforms GPTQ and approaches BF16 quality at 4-bit.

**HuggingFace SafeTensors (F16/BF16)**
The native HuggingFace format. Full precision or minimal quantization.

Characteristics:
- Best quality (no quantization loss)
- Largest files (7B F16 = 14GB, 70B F16 = 140GB)
- Multi-file shard format (model.safetensors.index.json + shard files)
- Supported by: vLLM (native), text-generation-webui (transformers backend)
- Can be loaded with torch, transformers, vLLM

When to use: Maximum quality is required, you have sufficient VRAM to run F16 (needs ~2× more VRAM than Q4), or you are fine-tuning (training requires full precision).

**Format Selection Decision Matrix**:

| Scenario | Recommended Format | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Apple Silicon + Ollama | GGUF Q4_K_M | Only format with full Metal support |
| CPU-only server | GGUF Q4_K_M | Only format with good CPU support |
| NVIDIA GPU, Ollama | GGUF Q4_K_M | Simplest, nearly as fast |
| NVIDIA GPU, vLLM, quality first | AWQ 4-bit | Best quality at 4-bit on CUDA |
| NVIDIA GPU, vLLM, any quality | SafeTensors BF16 | If VRAM permits |
| Research / fine-tuning | SafeTensors F32/BF16 | Training requires full precision |
| Maximum GPU throughput, GPTQ | GPTQ 4-bit ExLlamaV2 | Peak tokens/sec on GPU |

**Conversion**: Models can be converted between formats. HuggingFace → GGUF via llama.cpp convert scripts. GGUF → HuggingFace not straightforward. Always start with HuggingFace if you need flexibility.

title: Enterprise Security Configuration for Private AI Servers
content:
Exposing a local LLM server to a team network without proper security creates significant risks: unrestricted access, prompt injection, data exfiltration, and compliance failures. This guide covers the security baseline for private AI deployments.

**Network Security**

Never expose Ollama or vLLM directly on port 11434/8000 to your network without authentication. Both bind to all interfaces if configured for network access and have no built-in authentication.

Required network security stack:
1. Reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, or Caddy) as the only externally exposed point
2. TLS termination at the proxy (let's encrypt for internal PKI or company CA cert)
3. Authentication layer (see below)
4. Rate limiting to prevent abuse

nginx security config:
```nginx
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    ssl_protocols TLSv1.3;
    ssl_ciphers HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5;
    
    # API key validation
    if ($http_authorization != 'Bearer YOUR_SECRET_KEY') {
        return 401;
    }
    
    # Rate limiting
    limit_req zone=ai_limit burst=20 nodelay;
    
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:11434;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_read_timeout 300s;
        proxy_buffering off;
        
        # Remove user IP from upstream logs
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For '';
    }
}

limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=ai_limit:10m rate=10r/m;
```

**Authentication Options (by enterprise maturity)**:

Tier 1 — API Key (minimal):
- Static bearer token in nginx/Traefik
- Rotate quarterly
- One key per team/project for audit trail
- Tools: nginx `if ($http_authorization != 'Bearer...')`, Traefik BasicAuth middleware

Tier 2 — OAuth2/OIDC (recommended for teams):
- OAuth2 Proxy in front of the LLM server
- Integrates with Google Workspace, Microsoft Azure AD, Okta
- Per-user access logs and rate limits
- Example: `oauth2-proxy --upstream=http://localhost:11434 --provider=google`

Tier 3 — Kong/API Gateway (enterprise):
- Full API management: rate limiting per user, usage tracking, billing
- JWT validation, mTLS, IP allowlisting
- Audit logs for every request
- Plugins: `rate-limiting`, `key-auth`, `jwt`, `prometheus`

**Prompt Injection Defense**:
- System prompt hardening: Set a firm system prompt that instructs the model not to reveal its configuration or follow user attempts to change its role
- Input validation: Reject requests with known injection patterns (jailbreak attempts)
- Output filtering: Scan responses for PII, secrets (regex or secondary model classification)
- Audit logging: Log all requests/responses to immutable storage for compliance review

**Data Compliance (GDPR, HIPAA)**:
- Local inference = no data leaves your network (the core benefit)
- Audit logs may contain PHI/PII — treat them with appropriate access controls
- Log retention policy must match your compliance requirements
- Model weights stored locally = no vendor access to your models or data
- Document your data flow for compliance officers: User request → nginx (logged) → Ollama (processed locally) → response

**Container Security**:
```yaml
# Docker security hardening
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
    read_only: true
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp
    volumes:
      - ollama-models:/root/.ollama:ro  # Read-only model volume
    user: '1000:1000'  # Non-root user
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE
```

title: Monitoring and Observability for Local LLM Servers
content:
Without monitoring, you cannot detect performance degradation, capacity exhaustion, or failures in your private AI deployment. This guide covers the minimal viable observability stack and key metrics to track.

**Key Metrics to Monitor**

Throughput metrics:
- Tokens per second (generation speed) — primary performance indicator
- Requests per minute — traffic volume
- Concurrent active requests — concurrency level
- Queue depth — how many requests are waiting

Latency metrics:
- Time to first token (TTFT) — how long until streaming starts (prefill phase)
- Inter-token latency (ITL) — time between tokens (generation phase)
- End-to-end request latency — total response time
- P50, P95, P99 latency — tail latency for SLA compliance

Resource metrics:
- GPU VRAM utilization — critical; OOM = crash
- GPU compute utilization — should be high during inference, low otherwise
- CPU utilization — important for CPU inference
- Memory (RAM) utilization — watch for KV cache growth
- Disk I/O — model loading and logging impact

**Prometheus Metrics Collection**

vLLM exposes Prometheus metrics natively at /metrics:
```yaml
# Prometheus scrape config
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'vllm'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:8000']
    metrics_path: /metrics
```

Key vLLM Prometheus metrics:
- `vllm:num_requests_running` — active requests
- `vllm:num_requests_waiting` — queue depth
- `vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc` — KV cache utilization (>90% = pressure)
- `vllm:request_success_total` — successful completions
- `vllm:e2e_request_latency_seconds` — full request latency histogram
- `vllm:generation_tokens_total` — total tokens generated

Ollama does not expose Prometheus natively. Options:
- ollama-prometheus-exporter (community): `docker run -p 9090:9090 ollama-exporter`
- Custom exporter polling `curl localhost:11434/api/ps` for loaded models and status
- Application-level metrics: wrap Ollama API calls with timing in your application

**Grafana Dashboard (Essential Panels)**:
```json
{
  "panels": [
    {"title": "Tokens/sec", "query": "rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[1m])"},
    {"title": "Active Requests", "query": "vllm:num_requests_running"},
    {"title": "Queue Depth", "query": "vllm:num_requests_waiting"},
    {"title": "KV Cache %", "query": "vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc * 100"},
    {"title": "P95 Latency", "query": "histogram_quantile(0.95, vllm:e2e_request_latency_seconds_bucket)"},
    {"title": "GPU Utilization", "query": "nvidia_smi_utilization_gpu_ratio"},
    {"title": "VRAM Used", "query": "nvidia_smi_memory_used_bytes"}
  ]
}
```

**GPU Monitoring (nvidia-smi exporter)**:
```bash
# Install DCGM exporter for Kubernetes
helm install dcgm-exporter nvidia/dcgm-exporter

# Or standalone prometheus-dcgm-exporter
docker run -p 9400:9400 --runtime nvidia --gpus all \
  nvcr.io/nvidia/k8s/dcgm-exporter:latest
```

**Alerting Rules**:
```yaml
groups:
- name: llm_alerts
  rules:
  - alert: HighQueueDepth
    expr: vllm:num_requests_waiting > 10
    for: 2m
    annotations:
      summary: 'LLM queue > 10 requests — consider adding capacity'
  - alert: HighKVCacheUtilization
    expr: vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc > 0.90
    for: 5m
    annotations:
      summary: 'KV cache >90% — reduce max-model-len or add VRAM'
  - alert: LowTokensPerSecond
    expr: rate(vllm:generation_tokens_total[5m]) < 10
    for: 3m
    annotations:
      summary: 'Generation speed below 10 t/s — possible GPU issue'
```

**Log Management**:
- Ollama logs: `journalctl -u ollama -f` on systemd systems
- vLLM logs: structured JSON to stdout, capture with Fluentd/Loki
- Minimum log retention: 30 days for ops, 1 year for compliance
- PII scrubbing: Remove or hash user prompts before long-term storage

title: Cost Analysis: On-Premises Private AI vs Cloud API Pricing
content:
The financial case for private AI deployment depends on usage volume, data sensitivity requirements, and hardware lifecycle assumptions. This analysis provides a framework for the build-vs-buy decision.

**Cloud API Costs (Reference, 2025)**

OpenAI GPT-4o:
- Input: $2.50 per 1M tokens
- Output: $10.00 per 1M tokens
- At 50% input/50% output mix: $6.25 per 1M tokens average

Anthropic Claude Sonnet 3.5:
- Input: $3.00 per 1M tokens
- Output: $15.00 per 1M tokens

Meta Llama API (cloud, via providers):
- Llama-3.1-70B: ~$0.90-1.20 per 1M tokens

**Break-Even Analysis**

Scenario: Team of 20 developers using AI coding assistance, generating 2M tokens/day (40K tokens/developer/day — realistic for active coding use).

Cloud cost (GPT-4o):
- 2M tokens/day × $6.25/M = $12.50/day
- Monthly: $375
- Annual: $4,500

Private deployment option A — Mac Studio M3 Ultra (192GB):
- Hardware: $7,000 (one-time)
- Electricity: $50/month (40W × 24hr × 30 × $0.12/kWh)
- Maintenance: $100/month (conservative estimate)
- Total Year 1: $7,000 + $1,800 = $8,800
- Total Year 2: $1,800
- Total Year 3: $1,800
- 3-year total: $12,400
- Break-even vs cloud: Month 17

Private deployment option B — Used RTX 4090 Workstation:
- Hardware: $3,500 (workstation + 4090)
- Electricity: $130/month (450W average load × 24hr × 30 × $0.12)
- Total Year 1: $3,500 + $1,560 = $5,060
- 3-year total: $9,200
- Break-even vs cloud: Month 13

**Scaling Factor**: At 10M tokens/day (100-person team):
- Cloud cost: $62,500/month = $750,000/year
- Private (vLLM on 4× A100): hardware $60,000 + $500/month electricity
- Break-even: Month 2
- 3-year savings vs cloud: $2.2M

**Hidden Cost Factors**:

Private deployment additional costs:
- IT staff time for setup and maintenance: 2-4 hours/month for small deployments
- Hardware failure risk: GPU failure rate ~2-5% per year; budget for replacement
- Model licensing: Most open-source models are free; some (Llama-3 community license) restrict commercial use above certain scale
- Opportunity cost of capital tied up in hardware

Cloud API hidden costs:
- Data egress: Your prompts and responses leave your network — compliance cost in regulated industries
- Rate limits: Throttling during peak usage disrupts workflows
- API instability: Model versions deprecated with 30-90 days notice, breaking integrations
- Vendor lock-in: Migrating prompt engineering from one vendor to another has real labor cost

**When Cloud Wins**:
- Volume < 500K tokens/month (hardware cost dominates)
- Need cutting-edge frontier models (GPT-4o, Claude Opus) that exceed open-source quality
- Regulatory environment prohibits on-prem hardware ownership
- Team lacks DevOps capacity to run infrastructure

**When On-Premises Wins**:
- Regulated data (HIPAA, GDPR, financial data) that cannot leave your network
- Volume > 5M tokens/month (cloud becomes very expensive)
- Need to customize/fine-tune models on proprietary data
- Air-gapped environments where internet access is restricted

title: Model Selection Guide for Private AI: Open-Source Models by Use Case
content:
The quality of your private AI deployment depends as much on model selection as hardware. This guide maps use cases to the best available open-source models in 2025-2026.

**Coding and Software Development**

Best models:
- Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct: State-of-the-art for code as of late 2024-2025. Outperforms GPT-4o on many coding benchmarks. Available in GGUF format. Requires 20GB+ VRAM at Q4.
- DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Instruct (16B): Strong code model, reasonable size for consumer hardware.
- Codestral-22B (Mistral): Excellent for code completion, supports 32K context. 22B Q4 = 13GB.
- Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: Not specialized for code but highly capable general assistant for quick tasks.

**General-Purpose Chat and Q&A**

- Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct: Best open-source general-purpose model in its class. Approaches GPT-4 quality. Requires 48GB+ VRAM at Q4 or 70GB+ unified memory.
- Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: Excellent quality-to-size ratio. Runs on consumer hardware (8GB VRAM). Default recommendation for individual users.
- Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3: Fast, capable, 7B. Strong context understanding.
- Gemma-2-27B: Google's model, strong reasoning, available in GGUF.

**Document Analysis and RAG**

- Llama-3.1-8B or 70B: Strong context understanding, long context (128K tokens) support.
- Mistral-7B or Mistral-Nemo-12B: Efficient for RAG workloads, fast inference.
- Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct: Excellent at following complex instructions for document summarization.

**Multilingual Deployments**

- Qwen2.5-7B/14B/72B: Strongest multilingual support among open models. 29 languages natively.
- Mistral-Nemo-12B: Strong European language support (French, German, Spanish, Italian).
- Gemma-2-9B: Good multilingual, efficient size.

**Math and Reasoning**

- DeepSeek-R1 (distilled variants): Strong reasoning capabilities, available in 7B-70B sizes.
- Llama-3.1-70B: Good general reasoning, enhanced with chain-of-thought prompting.
- Qwen2.5-Math: Specialized math model from Alibaba.

**Embedding Models (for RAG and Search)**

- nomic-embed-text (via Ollama): 137M params, 768 dimensions, excellent retrieval quality.
- all-MiniLM-L6-v2: 22M params, 384 dimensions, fast, good quality-speed tradeoff.
- mxbai-embed-large: 335M params, 1024 dimensions, top-tier retrieval quality.
- BGE-M3: Multi-lingual, multi-function (dense + sparse + multi-vector).

**Model Sizing by Hardware** (recommended defaults):
| Hardware | Recommended Model | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| 8GB VRAM | Llama-3.1-8B Q4_K_M | Mistral-7B Q4_K_M |
| 16GB VRAM | Llama-3.1-8B Q8 or 13B Q4 | Codestral-22B Q2 |
| 24GB VRAM | Llama-3.1-8B F16 or 34B Q4 | Qwen2.5-Coder-32B Q4 |
| 48GB VRAM | Llama-3.1-70B Q4_K_M | Qwen2.5-72B Q4 |
| 80GB VRAM | Llama-3.1-70B BF16 | Mixtral-8x22B |
| Mac M3 Max 36GB | Llama-3.1-34B Q4 | Qwen2.5-32B Q4 |
| Mac M3 Ultra 192GB | Llama-3.1-70B Q8 | Qwen2.5-72B Q5 |

title: Quantization Quality Tradeoffs: Choosing the Right Precision Level
content:
Quantization reduces model file size and memory usage by representing weights in lower precision. Every bit you remove saves memory but potentially reduces quality. This guide provides the data to make an informed tradeoff decision.

**Quality Degradation by Quantization Level**

Based on perplexity benchmarks (lower perplexity = better quality) for Llama-3.1-8B:
- F32 (32-bit full): Perplexity baseline (reference, 100% quality)
- F16 (16-bit): Perplexity +0.01% — effectively identical to F32
- Q8_0 (8-bit): Perplexity +0.03% — imperceptible quality loss
- Q5_K_M (5-bit K-quant): Perplexity +0.18% — minimal loss, excellent tradeoff
- Q4_K_M (4-bit K-quant): Perplexity +0.43% — small loss, the recommended default
- Q3_K_M (3-bit): Perplexity +1.2% — noticeable on benchmarks, acceptable for casual use
- Q2_K (2-bit): Perplexity +3-5% — significant degradation, use only when VRAM is severely limited
- IQ1_S (1.5-bit): Perplexity +10%+ — severe degradation, models often incoherent

**K-Quant vs Standard Quants**:
K-quants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q6_K) use mixed precision — applying lower quantization to less important weights and higher precision to attention and critical layers. For the same bit count, K-quants are typically 0.3-0.8% better quality than standard quants.
- Q4_0 vs Q4_K_M: K_M is measurably better; always prefer K_M if your server supports it
- Q4_K_S vs Q4_K_M: K_M is slightly larger (~5% more) but better quality; prefer K_M

**Impact on Inference Speed**:
Quantization also affects speed:
- Q4_K_M vs F16: Q4_K_M is 1.5-2x faster on CPU (less memory bandwidth), roughly equal on GPU (GPU compute-bound at lower quants)
- Q8_0 vs F16: Q8_0 is ~1.3x faster on CPU, similar on GPU
- On memory-bandwidth-constrained hardware (Apple Silicon, older GPUs): lower quantization = faster

**Decision Framework**:
1. Start with Q4_K_M as your default — it's the best quality/size tradeoff for the vast majority of use cases
2. Upgrade to Q5_K_M or Q6_K if: VRAM permits, you're seeing quality issues, or output will be read by humans for high-stakes decisions
3. Upgrade to Q8_0 if: benchmarks show meaningful quality difference on your specific task and you have VRAM headroom
4. Use F16 only if: running a production service where quality is paramount and you have abundant VRAM
5. Drop to Q3_K_M only if: you must fit a model that otherwise won't load, and you accept quality reduction

**GGUF Naming Convention**:
`model-name-Q4_K_M.gguf`
- Q = quantized
- 4 = bits per weight (approximately)
- K = K-quant method
- M = medium variant (balance of size vs quality within the K-quant family)
- S = small variant (smaller, faster, slightly lower quality)
- L = large variant (larger, slower, slightly higher quality)

**EXL2 (text-generation-webui ExLlamaV2)**: A more sophisticated quantization method that outperforms GPTQ and approaches GGUF Q6_K quality at Q4 bit budgets, but only on NVIDIA CUDA. EXL2 at 4.0 bpw ≈ GGUF Q4_K_M in quality, with 10-20% faster generation on CUDA.
